subject: Physics
topic: Wave Properties and the Wave Equation

questions.q: What does a wave transfer?
questions.a: Energy (and information), without transferring matter.
questions.hint: A floating object stays roughly in place as a wave passes — what moves through it?
questions.explanation: This is fundamental: the wave moves through the medium, but the medium itself doesn't travel with it. A duck on the sea bobs up and down but doesn't move with the waves.

questions.q: Define the amplitude of a wave.
questions.a: The maximum displacement of a point on the wave from its rest position.
questions.hint: Measured from the centre line to the peak, not peak to trough.
questions.explanation: Amplitude is related to the energy carried by the wave — larger amplitude means more energy. Be careful not to confuse it with the peak-to-trough distance, which is twice the amplitude.

questions.q: Define the wavelength of a wave, and give its symbol.
questions.a: The distance from one point on a wave to the equivalent point on the next wave (e.g., crest to crest, or trough to trough). Symbol: λ (lambda). Measured in metres.
questions.hint: Symbol is a Greek letter that looks like an upside-down y.
questions.explanation: You can measure wavelength from any point to its equivalent on the next cycle — crest to crest, trough to trough, or any matching pair. It's a complete repetition distance.

questions.q: Define the frequency of a wave, and give its unit.
questions.a: The number of complete wave cycles passing a fixed point per second. Unit: hertz (Hz).
questions.hint: 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.
questions.explanation: Frequency is purely about how often the wave repeats. It doesn't tell you about speed or wavelength directly — but combined with wavelength via the wave equation, it gives you the wave speed.

questions.q: Define the time period of a wave.
questions.a: The time taken for one complete wave cycle to pass a fixed point. Measured in seconds (s).
questions.hint: It's the inverse of frequency.
questions.explanation: Time period and frequency are related by T = 1/f. A wave of frequency 50 Hz has a period of 1/50 = 0.02 s — each cycle takes 20 milliseconds.

questions.q: What is the wave equation?
questions.a: wave speed = frequency × wavelength, or v = f × λ.
questions.hint: Speed equals frequency times wavelength.
questions.explanation: This is the most important equation in the waves topic. Units must match: if frequency is in Hz and wavelength in metres, speed is in m/s. Make sure you can rearrange it: f = v/λ and λ = v/f.

questions.q: What is the relationship between frequency and time period?
questions.a: f = 1/T (frequency equals one divided by the time period), and equivalently T = 1/f.
questions.hint: They are reciprocals of one another.
questions.explanation: Higher frequency = shorter period (more cycles fit into each second). A 100 Hz wave has a period of 0.01 s; a 0.5 Hz wave has a period of 2 s.

questions.q: What is the difference between a transverse and a longitudinal wave?
questions.a: In a transverse wave, the oscillations are perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of energy transfer. In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer.
questions.hint: Think about how the medium vibrates relative to the wave's direction.
questions.explanation: Transverse: water waves, light, waves on a stretched rope. Longitudinal: sound, P-waves (primary seismic waves), Slinky compression waves. The wave equation applies to both types.

questions.q: Give two examples of transverse waves and two examples of longitudinal waves.
questions.a: Transverse: light (and all electromagnetic waves); water waves; waves on a stretched string or rope; S-waves (secondary seismic waves). Longitudinal: sound waves; P-waves (primary seismic waves); compression waves on a Slinky.
questions.hint: Light is transverse; sound is longitudinal.
questions.explanation: A common exam mistake is to confuse the two. Sound is definitely longitudinal — air molecules vibrate back and forth along the direction the sound is travelling, creating regions of compression and rarefaction.

questions.q: What is meant by the terms 'compression' and 'rarefaction' in a longitudinal wave?
questions.a: Compression: a region where particles of the medium are squashed closer together. Rarefaction: a region where particles are spread further apart.
questions.hint: One is high-pressure, the other is low-pressure.
questions.explanation: Compressions and rarefactions are the longitudinal-wave equivalent of crests and troughs in a transverse wave. The wavelength of a longitudinal wave can be measured from one compression to the next, or one rarefaction to the next.

questions.q: A water wave has a frequency of 2 Hz and a wavelength of 1.5 m. Calculate its speed.
questions.hint: Use v = f × λ.
questions.explanation: Straightforward substitution. Always check units: Hz × m gives m/s, which is the correct unit for speed.

questions.q: A sound wave has a speed of 340 m/s and a frequency of 1700 Hz. Calculate its wavelength.
questions.hint: Rearrange v = f × λ to get λ = v / f.
questions.explanation: This is a typical pitch for a human voice. Higher frequencies give shorter wavelengths at the same speed, which is why high notes have very short wavelengths in air.

questions.q: A wave has a frequency of 50 Hz. What is its time period?
questions.a: T = 1/f = 1/50 = 0.02 s (20 milliseconds).
questions.hint: Time period is the reciprocal of frequency.
questions.explanation: Mains electricity in the UK is 50 Hz — meaning the voltage completes a cycle every 20 ms. Useful for thinking about how oscilloscope traces of mains voltage look.

questions.q: What is diffraction?
questions.a: The spreading out of a wave as it passes through a gap or around an edge of an obstacle.
questions.hint: It's a 'spreading out' effect.
questions.explanation: Diffraction is why you can hear someone speaking around a corner even when you can't see them — sound diffracts more than light because its wavelength is much longer.

questions.q: Describe how the amount of diffraction depends on the size of the gap relative to the wavelength.
questions.a: Diffraction is greatest when the gap is roughly the same size as the wavelength. If the gap is much larger than the wavelength, only a small amount of diffraction occurs at the edges. If the gap is much smaller than the wavelength, the wave barely passes through.
questions.hint: Match the gap size to the wavelength for maximum diffraction.
questions.explanation: This is why a doorway diffracts sound waves well (gap ~1 m, sound wavelength ~1 m for typical speech) but diffracts light hardly at all (light wavelength ~500 nm, much smaller than the gap).

questions.q: A wave's frequency is doubled while its speed stays the same. What happens to its wavelength?
questions.a: The wavelength is halved.
questions.hint: From v = fλ, if v stays the same and f doubles, λ must halve.
questions.explanation: Use the wave equation: if v is constant, then f and λ are inversely proportional. Double one, halve the other. This is a common conceptual question testing whether you can use the equation algebraically rather than just plug numbers in.

questions.q: Describe an experiment using a ripple tank to show that all waves can be diffracted through a gap.
questions.a: Set up a ripple tank with a vibrating dipper or bar to create straight waves of known wavelength. Place a barrier with an adjustable gap in the path of the waves. Observe the wave pattern after the gap from above (using a lamp shining down through the water onto a screen). Vary the gap size from much larger than the wavelength down to about one wavelength: diffraction (spreading) increases as the gap size approaches the wavelength.
questions.hint: Ripple tank, barrier with a gap, observe spreading.
questions.explanation: This is a standard required practical demonstration. The same equipment can be used to demonstrate reflection (by tilting a barrier) and refraction (by placing a glass plate in the tank to make a shallower area). It is a core method for visualising wave behaviour.

questions.q: A water wave has a wavelength of 4 m and a time period of 0.5 s. Calculate its speed.
questions.a: First find frequency: f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz. Then v = f × λ = 2 × 4 = 8 m/s.
questions.hint: Find frequency first using f = 1/T, then use v = f × λ.
questions.explanation: A two-step problem: typical higher-tier question. Always work in SI units (Hz, m, s) and check that your final unit makes sense.

questions.q: Explain why the speed of sound in air is approximately constant for all frequencies, even though high-pitched sounds have very different wavelengths from low-pitched sounds.
questions.a: The speed of sound in air depends on the properties of the medium (temperature, pressure, density) rather than on the wave's frequency. Because air at a given temperature has a fixed speed of sound (~340 m/s at room temperature), higher frequencies simply correspond to shorter wavelengths, in accordance with v = f × λ. Both a high note and a low note travel through the air at the same speed.
questions.hint: What determines the speed of sound in a medium?
questions.explanation: If sound speed varied with frequency, then a music performance heard from across a concert hall would arrive as a jumbled mess as high notes arrived at a different time from low notes. This is also true of light in a vacuum (but light slightly disperses by frequency in glass — which is how a prism works).

questions.q: A student is told that a particular wave has a frequency of 5 Hz, a wavelength of 0.6 m, and a speed of 2 m/s. Explain whether these values are consistent.
questions.a: Use the wave equation: v = f × λ should hold. Calculated value: 5 × 0.6 = 3 m/s, not 2 m/s. The values are inconsistent — the wave speed cannot simultaneously be 2 m/s if the frequency is 5 Hz and the wavelength is 0.6 m. At least one of the measurements is wrong.
questions.hint: Plug the numbers into v = f × λ and check whether they match.
questions.explanation: Exam questions sometimes include deliberate inconsistencies to test whether you can apply the equation critically. The wave equation is a definitional relationship — it must always hold for a given wave.
